frames.caption: Heapify [8, 5, 3, 1] into a min-heap in one O(n) pass. Start at the last non-leaf, index 1.
frames.note: Floyd build-heap heapifies an arbitrary array in ONE pass instead of n separate O(log n) pushes. We never touch the leaves: a single node is already a valid heap, so the smallest index that can violate the invariant is the last NON-LEAF, at n/2 - 1. We sift-down each internal node, walking that index DOWN to 0. Processing bottom-up guarantees that when we sift a node, both its subtrees are already valid heaps.

frames.caption: SiftDown internal node at index 1 (value 5).
frames.note: Begin sift-down at this internal node. Bottom-up order matters: every node below it has already been fixed, so its left and right subtrees are valid heaps. The only possible violation is HERE — this node may be larger than one of its children — and sift-down pushes it down only as far as needed.

frames.caption: At index 1 (value 5): smaller child is a[3]=1.
frames.note: Sift-down must compare BOTH children and pick the SMALLER one (min-heap) — choosing the wrong child is the classic heap bug that breaks the invariant. Children of i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2; we guard each against n so we never read past the array. If neither child is smaller, this node is already in order and sift-down stops here.

frames.caption: Swap a[1] with smaller child a[3]=1 — sink it one level, continue at 3.
frames.note: The node is bigger than its smaller child, so swap them and continue from the child index — the value keeps sinking while it is out of order. Each swap moves it one level deeper, so a sift-down costs at most O(height) for THIS node, not O(log n) for the whole tree.

frames.caption: At index 3 (value 5): smaller child is neither — node is smallest.
frames.note: Sift-down must compare BOTH children and pick the SMALLER one (min-heap) — choosing the wrong child is the classic heap bug that breaks the invariant. Children of i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2; we guard each against n so we never read past the array. If neither child is smaller, this node is already in order and sift-down stops here.

frames.caption: a[3]=5 is <= both children: sift-down stops.
frames.note: Both children are now larger (or this is a leaf), so the node has found its place and sift-down stops. This is the crux of the O(n) bound: nodes near the bottom — which are the VAST majority — sift down only a level or two. Summing n/2^(h+1) nodes each doing O(h) work converges to 2n, so the whole build is O(n), not O(n log n).

frames.caption: Node 1 done — its subtree is now a valid min-heap (array: [8, 1, 3, 5]).
frames.note: Bottom-up the heap grows from the leaves up: after fixing index 1, every index >= 1 roots a valid heap. Both children are now larger (or this is a leaf), so the node has found its place and sift-down stops. This is the crux of the O(n) bound: nodes near the bottom — which are the VAST majority — sift down only a level or two. Summing n/2^(h+1) nodes each doing O(h) work converges to 2n, so the whole build is O(n), not O(n log n).

frames.caption: SiftDown internal node at index 0 (value 8).
frames.note: Begin sift-down at this internal node. Bottom-up order matters: every node below it has already been fixed, so its left and right subtrees are valid heaps. The only possible violation is HERE — this node may be larger than one of its children — and sift-down pushes it down only as far as needed.

frames.caption: At index 0 (value 8): smaller child is a[1]=1.
frames.note: Sift-down must compare BOTH children and pick the SMALLER one (min-heap) — choosing the wrong child is the classic heap bug that breaks the invariant. Children of i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2; we guard each against n so we never read past the array. If neither child is smaller, this node is already in order and sift-down stops here.

frames.caption: Swap a[0] with smaller child a[1]=1 — sink it one level, continue at 1.
frames.note: The node is bigger than its smaller child, so swap them and continue from the child index — the value keeps sinking while it is out of order. Each swap moves it one level deeper, so a sift-down costs at most O(height) for THIS node, not O(log n) for the whole tree.

frames.caption: At index 1 (value 8): smaller child is a[3]=5.
frames.note: Sift-down must compare BOTH children and pick the SMALLER one (min-heap) — choosing the wrong child is the classic heap bug that breaks the invariant. Children of i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2; we guard each against n so we never read past the array. If neither child is smaller, this node is already in order and sift-down stops here.

frames.caption: Swap a[1] with smaller child a[3]=5 — sink it one level, continue at 3.
frames.note: The node is bigger than its smaller child, so swap them and continue from the child index — the value keeps sinking while it is out of order. Each swap moves it one level deeper, so a sift-down costs at most O(height) for THIS node, not O(log n) for the whole tree.

frames.caption: At index 3 (value 8): smaller child is neither — node is smallest.
frames.note: Sift-down must compare BOTH children and pick the SMALLER one (min-heap) — choosing the wrong child is the classic heap bug that breaks the invariant. Children of i live at 2i+1 and 2i+2; we guard each against n so we never read past the array. If neither child is smaller, this node is already in order and sift-down stops here.

frames.caption: a[3]=8 is <= both children: sift-down stops.
frames.note: Both children are now larger (or this is a leaf), so the node has found its place and sift-down stops. This is the crux of the O(n) bound: nodes near the bottom — which are the VAST majority — sift down only a level or two. Summing n/2^(h+1) nodes each doing O(h) work converges to 2n, so the whole build is O(n), not O(n log n).

frames.caption: Node 0 done — its subtree is now a valid min-heap (array: [1, 5, 3, 8]).
frames.note: Bottom-up the heap grows from the leaves up: after fixing index 0, every index >= 0 roots a valid heap. Both children are now larger (or this is a leaf), so the node has found its place and sift-down stops. This is the crux of the O(n) bound: nodes near the bottom — which are the VAST majority — sift down only a level or two. Summing n/2^(h+1) nodes each doing O(h) work converges to 2n, so the whole build is O(n), not O(n log n).

frames.caption: Heapified in O(n): the min-heap is [1, 5, 3, 8] — root 1 is the minimum.
frames.note: Every internal node has been sifted down, so the array is now a valid min-heap: root 1 is the minimum, reachable in O(1). Building this way (Floyd, bottom-up) cost O(n); doing n separate pushes would have cost O(n log n). This is exactly what C#'s seeding constructor `new PriorityQueue<T,T>(items)` and `EnqueueRange` do under the hood.
